name: Budget Planning
description: RECOMMENDED APPROACH A — EXPLICIT VERSIONED MONTHLY PLAN SNAPSHOTS. For one unambiguous Budget Period, the owner creates a draft Budget Plan Revision, assigns exact ZAR amounts to stable active LEDGER Spend Category identifiers, sees exact totals plus explicit zero and omitted states, and activates one immutable revision. BUDGET may query only the public snapshot-bound LEDGER actuals contract to return a raw Budget Position: planned, actual, remaining or over, and explicit unbudgeted or uncategorized amounts. It does not forecast, judge pace, narrate, or render reports. A later change creates another attributable revision; prior intent remains inspectable. Complexity M and within the existing local-first budget: one in-process CLI module, at most one module-local store, no service, scheduler, network, or generic policy engine. Root-problem completeness 9/10. It builds directly on G3, G5, and G6 of LEDGER and supplies the hard BUDGET dependency already recorded for INSIGHTS. Pros: deterministic, auditable, category-aligned, and a stable target for later analysis. Cons: the owner must maintain explicit monthly amounts and category lifecycle requires careful handling. Biggest risk: incomplete categorization or ambiguous revision timing can make an exact calculation look more trustworthy than its inputs. Stress test: every active actual in a representative month must be accounted for as budgeted, zero-budget, unbudgeted, or uncategorized without copying finance semantics out of LEDGER.

ALTERNATIVE B — RECURRING POLICY AND ROLLOVER ENGINE. Define standing category limits, percentage allocations, pay-cycle rules, sinking funds, automatic carry-over, and exceptions that generate each month. Complexity H and outside the M budget; root-problem completeness 10/10 if the policy semantics are correct. It reduces repetitive entry and can model richer household planning, but inheritance, retroactive edits, income timing, rollover debt, and category changes create hidden effective values and a much larger test matrix. Biggest risk: automation silently changes the effective plan or produces a target the owner did not consciously approve. Rejected for v1; recurrence and rollover remain discovery evidence for a later reframe, not speculative scope now.

ALTERNATIVE C — DO LESS WITH AN EXTERNAL SPREADSHEET OR CANONICAL TARGET FILE. Keep the monthly plan outside Tally and let the owner or AI Agent Host supply category targets to an INSIGHTS invocation after validation against LEDGER categories. Complexity L and within budget; root-problem completeness 5/10. It avoids a new durable store and may be sufficient if the owner changes targets rarely. It loses authoritative history, activation state, consistent period semantics, and safe reuse across agents, but remains the correct fallback if persistent BUDGET adds no demonstrated value. Biggest risk: target files drift from category identity and each report reconstructs plan semantics differently.

SELECTION. Choose Approach A because it is the smallest durable contract that makes planned intent as reproducible as actuals. The non-obvious inversion is that a budget is a versioned plan snapshot, not a live analytics engine: BUDGET owns exact intent and raw comparison, while INSIGHTS owns pace, projection, trend, anomaly, and narrative. Approach B loses on complexity and hidden automation; Approach C loses on provenance and reusable semantics but remains the kill-criterion outcome. No new project ADR is needed because the selection preserves the accepted local in-process, structured CLI, public-contract architecture.

SCOPE SIGNALS AND ROUTING. Signals present: sensitive local financial plans; external integration with the public LEDGER contract and AI Agent Host; a cross-module flow from LEDGER through BUDGET to INSIGHTS; a likely model spanning periods, plans, revisions, category entries, activation, and budget positions; and significant unknowns around monthly boundaries, plan completeness, revision timing, category lifecycle, and the usefulness of copy-forward or rollover. Five signals are present, with sensitive financial data and significant unknowns independently high-impact. Route to /lex:discovery BUDGET before /lex:prd.
DISCOVERY v0.2 — ACTORS AND CURRENT STATE

The sole actor is P1 Personal Finance Owner, sometimes delegating through an authorized local AI Agent Host under the same operating-system identity. Today the owner inspects separate statements, remembers or externally records category targets, overwrites or copies spreadsheet-like plans without stable activation history, and manually reconciles category changes. There is no machine-readable distinction between draft, active, and restated intent.
DESIRED WORKFLOWS

The owner drafts an immutable current or future calendar-month category plan; explicitly activates it with atomic supersession; inspects an exact snapshot-bound plan-versus-actual position; reconstructs plan revision history; and discovers the same bounded operations through an agent contract. Dedicated copy-previous behavior is deferred: the Agent Host may read a prior revision and submit an ordinary explicit draft.
PRE-MORTEM

The most likely failure modes are false confidence caused by uncategorized or misclassified actuals (RISK-001); category, period, or revision semantics invalidating historical meaning (RISK-002, RISK-003, RISK-004); and upkeep burden leaving plans stale or abandoned (RISK-006). Boundary duplication and privacy leakage remain covered by RISK-007 and RISK-008.
DOMAIN DISPOSITIONS
Authorization — CHECK: one owner and one trusted local OS identity; explicit mutation authority; shared or hosted use reopens the model.
Data model and persistence — DEEP: stable Budget Plan identity per period; immutable Draft, Active, and Superseded revisions; atomic replay-safe activation; closed completed periods.
UI and interaction — N/A: v1 is capability and contract scope, with no dedicated graphical interface.
Integrations — DEEP: LEDGER supplies category lifecycle and exact snapshot-bound actuals; Agent Host invokes owner-authorized operations; INSIGHTS consumes raw plan and position data.
Background processing — N/A: every v1 operation is explicit and synchronous; there are no scheduled rollovers or automatic activation jobs.
Reporting and analytics — DEEP: BUDGET returns exact raw positions and provenance only; INSIGHTS owns pace, forecast, trend, anomaly, recommendation, narrative, and durable report snapshots.
Notifications — N/A: alerts, reminders, and nudges are excluded from v1.
Configuration and administration — DEEP: category entry validation, draft lifecycle, explicit activation, period closure, and revision inspection are owner administration workflows.
Multi-tenancy — N/A: the validated boundary is one owner and one local OS identity.

Financial semantics — DEEP: non-negative exact ZAR planned amounts; explicit zero differs from omission; independent categories with a computed total; actuals inherit LEDGER transfer, refund, correction, allocation, void, and supersession semantics verbatim.
Security and privacy — DEEP: fail closed, owner-only local files, explicit data return only, and no financial payloads in Git, logs, crash diagnostics, process arguments, or shared temporary files.

Compliance — CHECK: current purely personal or household local processing is excluded by POPIA section 6(1)(a); cloud, shared-user, third-party, or commercial expansion requires a new assessment. Source: https://www.justice.gov.za/legislation/acts/2013-004.pdf
INTEGRATED RULES AND EXAMPLES

A Budget Period is the Ledger Effective Date interval from the first calendar date inclusive to the next month first date exclusive. A ZAR 0.00 entry means Zero Budget; omission means Unbudgeted. No active plan, an empty plan, an all-zero plan, and a plan with no actuals are distinct states. Category renames preserve stable identity; archived categories remain readable in history but cannot receive new or current entries; reactivation does not revive a former amount. Budget Position recomputes current corrected LEDGER actuals for a cited snapshot and retains no separate actual snapshot.
INTEGRATION, SECURITY, AND FEASIBILITY

The LEDGER, Agent Host, host OS, and INSIGHTS contracts are recorded as assumed external dependencies with explicit direction, data, authority, and failure boundaries. Selective STRIDE analysis emphasized tampering, repudiation, and information disclosure; spoofing and elevation remain bounded by the single-owner OS identity, and denial-of-service is recoverable because operations are local and replay-safe. All goals are known or complex with medium-high to high confidence; no low-confidence technical uncertainty requires a code spike. Runtime conformance of the LEDGER and host contracts remains a later implementation gate.
ASSUMPTION AND QUESTION BASELINE

A1 through A7 record calendar periods, category amount semantics, closure, current corrected actuals, reusable host protections, maintenance value, and single-owner identity. OQ-BUDGET-1 through OQ-BUDGET-5 and OQ-BUDGET-7 through OQ-BUDGET-8 are resolved. OQ-BUDGET-6 is resolved for v1: no dedicated copy-previous operation; the representative 15-minute maintenance metric remains a monitored trigger for a future reframe.
KILL AND SCOPE RECHECK

No kill criterion is currently triggered. No newly discovered workflow requires v1 scope growth. If spend-only planning is not useful, exact LEDGER actuals are unavailable, deterministic replay is impossible, maintenance exceeds the threshold without a small remedy, or safe local persistence is infeasible, the existing kill criteria require stopping or reframing before implementation.
problem_statement: Surface pain: trusted LEDGER actuals can show what was spent, but the owner still cannot tell whether monthly category spending aligns with intent because planned amounts live outside the same category and period model. 5 Whys: Why create monthly budgets? To know whether spending is on track before the month ends. Why are actuals insufficient? Actuals have no owner-approved target. Why can a spreadsheet or remembered amount not supply the target reliably? Its category identity, period boundary, revisions, and zero-versus-omitted meaning are detached from canonical LEDGER facts. Why does that matter downstream? BUDGET and INSIGHTS can otherwise select different periods or silently omit uncategorized and unbudgeted spend. Why does the mismatch persist? Tally has no authoritative, agent-operable plan contract that preserves monthly intent and composes with snapshot-consistent actuals. Root problem: Tally lacks a local, versioned monthly spending-plan baseline over stable LEDGER Spend Categories, so the owner must manually reconstruct intent and downstream on-track analysis cannot be reproducible. Without BUDGET, the owner continues comparing statements and remembered limits by hand, revisions lose provenance, and INSIGHTS has no trustworthy target. Journey: the sole Personal Finance Owner uses an AI Agent Host to discover BUDGET operations, creates or revises a monthly category plan, reviews exact totals and explicit omissions, activates it, and queries a snapshot-bound budget position while interpretation remains downstream.
why_now: LEDGER now defines stable Spend Categories and snapshot-consistent actuals, while INGEST and CLASSIFY define how complete categorized transactions arrive. INSIGHTS is the next downstream module and cannot be framed safely until monthly plan, revision, period, and unbudgeted-spend semantics are explicit.

rationale: Monthly category budgets require the ledger's shared spend categories and transaction actuals.
